name: Dana Okonkwo
bio: Former mayor who rebuilt a struggling city on public investment. Running on the idea that strong shared systems create opportunity.
background: Two-term mayor of a mid-sized industrial city. Built a municipal broadband network and a public apprenticeship program credited with cutting youth unemployment. Daughter of a transit worker and a nurse. Believes government should be a builder, not a bystander.

platformPlanks.title: A Health Care Floor No One Falls Through
platformPlanks.body: Guarantee a baseline of affordable coverage funded publicly, so a job loss never means losing care. Keep private options on top for those who want them.

platformPlanks.title: Public Broadband as Infrastructure
platformPlanks.body: Treat high-speed internet like roads and water: a public utility every community can reach. Fund municipal networks where markets won't build.

platformPlanks.title: Apprenticeships Over Debt
platformPlanks.body: Fund paid public apprenticeships in trades, care work, and clean energy so young people can build a career without a mountain of loans.

platformPlanks.title: Clean Energy, Public Jobs
platformPlanks.body: Pair a clean-energy buildout with a public jobs guarantee in the regions that powered the last century, so the transition lifts workers instead of leaving them.

sources.title: Inauguration as Mayor
sources.excerpt: A city is not its skyline. It's whether the bus comes, the clinic is open, and the kid down the block can picture a future here. That's the work.

sources.title: Why I Build
sources.excerpt: Markets are brilliant at many things and blind to others. Where they're blind — a rural clinic, a flood wall, a slow neighborhood — the public has to see.

sources.title: The Care Floor Plan
sources.excerpt: A funded baseline of coverage, automatic enrollment, and a cap on out-of-pocket costs. Private plans layer on top. No one is left uncovered between jobs.

name: Marcus Reed
bio: Constitutional lawyer and small-business owner who believes the surest protection of ordinary people is a government that stays in its lane.
background: Spent fifteen years defending civil-liberties cases, then ran a family hardware chain. Skeptical of concentrated power whether it sits in an agency or a boardroom. Runs on a flatter tax code, hard limits on surveillance, and decisions made closest to the people they affect.

platformPlanks.title: Your Data Is Yours
platformPlanks.body: Warrant requirements for digital surveillance, a ban on buying around the Fourth Amendment, and a right to know what the government collects on you.

platformPlanks.title: A Tax Code on a Postcard
platformPlanks.body: Collapse the brackets, kill the loopholes the connected use, and let most families file in minutes. Simplicity is fairness.

platformPlanks.title: Decisions Closest to Home
platformPlanks.body: Push power down to states and towns wherever the Constitution allows. The people who live with a policy should be the ones who write it.

platformPlanks.title: Speech Without a Permission Slip
platformPlanks.body: Defend open expression against pressure to police lawful speech, online or off. The answer to bad speech is more speech, not a gatekeeper.

sources.title: The Quiet Erosion
sources.excerpt: Rights are rarely lost in one dramatic vote. They're traded away a convenience at a time, until the warrant is a formality and the dragnet is the default.

sources.title: Closing Argument, Carter v. State
sources.excerpt: The state does not get to search everyone to find the few. That is the whole point of the rule. Inconvenience to power is the feature, not the bug.

sources.title: The Flat Filing Plan
sources.excerpt: Two rates, a generous standard deduction, and the end of carve-outs written for lobbyists. Revenue-neutral, radically simpler, and honest about who pays.

name: Sofia Alvarez
bio: Climate engineer turned policymaker who runs on the numbers: invest now in the things that compound — energy, education, and safe technology.
background: Trained as an electrical engineer, led a national lab's grid-modernization program, then chaired a state energy commission. Allergic to slogans, fluent in tradeoffs. Believes the job of government is to make the long-term cheaper than the short-term.

platformPlanks.title: Safety Standards for Powerful AI
platformPlanks.body: Independent testing and disclosure for high-impact AI systems before deployment — the same way we test bridges and medicines. Innovation with guardrails.

platformPlanks.title: Cheap, Clean, and Built to Last
platformPlanks.body: Front-load research and grid upgrades so clean power is the cheapest option, not the virtuous one. Make the math do the persuading.

platformPlanks.title: Protect Students' Data
platformPlanks.body: A federal floor for student-data privacy: clear consent, no selling of children's records, and audits with teeth. Ed-tech should serve kids, not harvest them.

platformPlanks.title: STEM Without a Zip-Code Lottery
platformPlanks.body: Fund labs, broadband, and teacher pipelines in the schools that have the least, so a kid's potential isn't capped by where they were born.

sources.title: The AI Assurance Framework
sources.excerpt: Tiered oversight by capability: the higher the potential impact, the higher the bar for testing, transparency, and recall. Low-risk tools stay unburdened.

sources.title: Answering a Student on Jobs
sources.excerpt: I won't tell you a robot will never do part of your job. I'll tell you we can decide what we do with the time it frees — and who gets the gains.

sources.title: The Cheapest Future Wins
sources.excerpt: Stop arguing about virtue. Make the clean, safe, durable choice the cheap one, and the market will sprint where the speeches couldn't drag it.

name: Hank Whitfield
bio: Third-generation farmer and county commissioner who thinks the country forgot its small towns — and that fixing that is national business.
background: Runs a family farm and chaired his county board for a decade. Watched the main street he grew up on hollow out and the broadband never arrive. Folksy, stubborn, and convinced that places — not just people — deserve a fair shot.

platformPlanks.title: Finish the Rural Broadband Job
platformPlanks.body: No more maps that pretend a town is covered when it isn't. Wire the last mile, with local co-ops in the driver's seat. A farm needs bandwidth like it needs rain.

platformPlanks.title: Main Street, Not Just Wall Street
platformPlanks.body: Tax and credit rules that favor the local hardware store and the family farm over the absentee chain. Keep the dollar circulating where it was earned.

platformPlanks.title: Decisions Made on the Porch
platformPlanks.body: Block one-size-fits-all mandates that treat a county of 4,000 like a city of four million. Trust towns to know their own roads and rivers.

platformPlanks.title: Keep the Lights On at the Local Clinic
platformPlanks.body: Stop rural hospitals from closing with steady funding and telehealth that actually reaches the holler. Care shouldn't be a two-hour drive.

sources.title: County Fair Remarks
sources.excerpt: Folks in the capital talk about flyover country. Well, we don't fly over our own homes. We live in them. And we're done being an afterthought.

sources.title: On the Broadband Maps
sources.excerpt: They colored my county green on the coverage map. I'd like to see them stream a thing out at my mailbox. Green on paper, dead air in real life.

sources.title: Places Deserve a Fair Shot
sources.excerpt: We spend a lot of breath on equal opportunity for people. I'd argue a town can be born without one too — and we can choose to fix that.

name: Patricia Vance
bio: Former federal judge running on steady hands: public safety, secure borders with due process, and institutions that work the same for everyone.
background: Spent twenty years on the bench after a career as a prosecutor. Believes predictability is a public good and that trust in institutions is the quiet thing holding everything else up. Measured by temperament, firm on order — and insistent that order without fairness is just force.

platformPlanks.title: Safe Streets, Fair Courts
platformPlanks.body: Fund community policing and the courts that hold it accountable in equal measure. Safety and fairness are not a trade — lose one and you lose both.

platformPlanks.title: Borders With Due Process
platformPlanks.body: Secure, orderly entry and a real, faster path through the line. A system no one trusts serves no one — not the newcomer, not the neighbor.

platformPlanks.title: Defend the Machinery of Trust
platformPlanks.body: Protect elections, courts, and the civil service from capture by any party or person. The referee can't wear a jersey.

platformPlanks.title: Lock the Digital Doors
platformPlanks.body: Treat cyberattacks on hospitals, grids, and schools as the threats they are. Fund defense, share warnings, and stop pretending it's someone else's problem.

sources.title: Investiture Address
sources.excerpt: A courtroom works because everyone, the powerful most of all, believes the rules will hold tomorrow. Guard that belief and you guard the republic.

sources.title: Order Without Fairness Is Just Force
sources.excerpt: I have signed warrants and overturned them. The lesson of both is the same: authority earns its weight only when it answers to a rule it cannot bend.

sources.title: The Civic Resilience Plan
sources.excerpt: Hardened election systems, protected inspectors general, and a cyber-defense corps for critical services. The unglamorous work of keeping trust intact.
